coredata.dc:title: A novel quaternary ammonium structure of carbon dots modified TiO2 for fast reduction of Cr(VI) over a wide pH range under sunlight
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zijun
Ma, Ruoxin
Zhang, Chun
Chen, Jiao
Fan, Jingbiao
Shi, Qingdong
coredata.dc:description:
Cr(VI) pollution in water caused by industrialization poses a serious threat to human health and ecosystems, necessitating a rapid reduction of Cr(VI) treatment method. Therefore, We have established a photocatalytic method for the rapid reduction of Cr(VI) by modifying TiO2 with carbon dots (CPC-CDs) that possess a quaternary ammonium structure in the first time. The CPC-CDs widens the band gap of TiO2 and increases the electrostatic interactions between CPC-CDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI), thus improving the reduction efficiency. CPC-CDs/TiO2 (0.1 g, 2 % CPC-CDs) exhibited excellent reduction performance and fully reduced Cr(VI) (50 mg/L) under neutral conditions within 150 min using a xenon lamp to simulate sunlight. Moreover, CPC-CDs/TiO2 achieved more than 95 % reduction efficiency over a wide pH range (pH = 3, 5, 7, and 9) and showed excellent reduction of Cr(VI) at various concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg/L). CPC-CDs/TiO2 maintained high Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (93.57 %) after five cycles. Tests under real sunlight revealed that CPC-CDs/TiO2 thoroughly reduced Cr(VI) (10 mg/L) within 30 min, and exhibited excellent structural stability under conditions with interfering ions. Furthermore, CPC-CDs/TiO2 exhibited prominent antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli. In addition, free-radical trapping experiments verified that the superoxide radicals (•
O
2
-
) was the main active species. This study aims to overcome the limitations of photocatalytic reduction under conditions of high concentration Cr(VI), neutral and alkaline environments, and natural sunlight, thereby providing technical support for the rapid reduction of Cr(VI).

head.ce:title: A novel quaternary ammonium structure of carbon dots modified TiO2 for fast reduction of Cr(VI) over a wide pH range under sunlight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Cr(VI) pollution in water caused by industrialization poses a serious threat to human health and ecosystems, necessitating a rapid reduction of Cr(VI) treatment method. Therefore, We have established a photocatalytic method for the rapid reduction of Cr(VI) by modifying TiO2 with carbon dots (CPC-CDs) that possess a quaternary ammonium structure in the first time. The CPC-CDs widens the band gap of TiO2 and increases the electrostatic interactions between CPC-CDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI), thus improving the reduction efficiency. CPC-CDs/TiO2 (0.1 g, 2 % CPC-CDs) exhibited excellent reduction performance and fully reduced Cr(VI) (50 mg/L) under neutral conditions within 150 min using a xenon lamp to simulate sunlight. Moreover, CPC-CDs/TiO2 achieved more than 95 % reduction efficiency over a wide pH range (pH = 3, 5, 7, and 9) and showed excellent reduction of Cr(VI) at various concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg/L). CPC-CDs/TiO2 maintained high Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (93.57 %) after five cycles. Tests under real sunlight revealed that CPC-CDs/TiO2 thoroughly reduced Cr(VI) (10 mg/L) within 30 min, and exhibited excellent structural stability under conditions with interfering ions. Furthermore, CPC-CDs/TiO2 exhibited prominent antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli. In addition, free-radical trapping experiments verified that the superoxide radicals (•
O
2
-
) was the main active species. This study aims to overcome the limitations of photocatalytic reduction under conditions of high concentration Cr(VI), neutral and alkaline environments, and natural sunlight, thereby providing technical support for the rapid reduction of Cr(VI).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of Cr(VI) in water poses a significant threat to the environment. Cr(VI) is lethal to fish and other aquatic organisms, and has the potential to disrupt self-purification mechanisms in aquatic environments. Additionally, the irrigation of agricultural fields with Cr-laden water inhibits the decomposition of soil organic matter, resulting in decreased agricultural productivity and ecological imbalance. Once Cr(VI) enters the ecological cycle, it poses a significant threat to human health [1]. The capacity of Cr(VI) to induce genetic mutations may lead to the development of malignant tumors and cancer. The International Agency for Research on Cancer categorized Cr(VI) as a Group (1) carcinogen because of its high toxicity, carcinogenicity, and mutagenicity. Cr(VI) contamination has emerged as a primary threat to human safety in regions with lenient industrial emission regulations [2]. Many advanced technologies with high efficiencies and low costs have been developed for treating Cr(VI) in water, including precipitation [3], adsorption [4], biological methods [5], ion exchange [6], membrane filtration [7], chemical reduction [8], electrocatalysis [9], and photocatalysis [10]. Among these methods, photocatalytic methods have demonstrated excellent potential for the photoreduction of Cr(VI), owing to their vital advantages such as low energy use, non-toxic, and simplicity.

Photocatalytic technology continuously converts solar energy into electrical energy to reduce Cr(VI) into safer lower-valence forms of Cr ions [11]. Photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) participate in redox reactions in the conduction and valence bands, respectively, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). For instance, highly reducing e− activate oxygen molecules (O2) in solution to generate •
O
2
-
. Highly oxidizing h+ interacts with water (H2O) and hydroxide ions (OH−) to produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [12]. These radicals migrated to the surface of the catalyst and reduce Cr(VI) [13]. Individual photocatalysts are constrained by the rapid recombination of e−–h+ pairs, which limits the solar energy utilization efficiency. Cheng et al. [14] developed a Bi4Ti3O1 /CdS composite with superior light-induced reduction activity compared to pure CdS. Sadra et al. [15] achieved 99.9 % reduction of Cr(VI) in 30 min under visible light at pH 2 using an iron(III)-based metal–organic framework fixed on sand surfaces. Wang et al. [16] enhanced the photocatalytic activity of Cr(VI) reduction by introducing Br-doped g-C3N4, which removed 61.6 % of the Cr(VI) in a 7 ppm solution within 2 h, achieving a twice enhancement over pure g-C3N4. However, based on existing research, photocatalytic methods for the reduction of Cr(VI) exhibit several limitations: (1) limited capability to reduce high concentrations of Cr(VI); (2) weak reduction efficiency under neutral and alkaline conditions; and (3) most studies have been conducted using laboratory light sources, limiting their applicability in real environmental conditions. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a photocatalyst capable of efficiently reducing high concentrations of Cr(VI) over a wide pH range under natural sunlight.

Carbon dots (CDs) offer a novel pathway for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency owing to their exceptional tailorability and outstanding optical properties [17]. Combining CDs with semiconductor photocatalysts can effectively broaden the light-absorption range and reduce the bandgap energy, leading to an enhanced photocatalytic performance. Specifically, CDs act as excellent electron donors and acceptors, enhancing the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges in individual photocatalysts. Their electron-transfer capabilities stem from sp2 hybridization [18]. In addition, the surface defects of CDs provide a unique upconversion photoluminescence effect that enables the conversion of ultraviolet light into visible light, facilitating the utilization of a broader range of the solar spectrum [19]. Moreover, CDs can be employed to enhance adsorption and wettability [20], improve photocatalytic activity by reducing the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Zhou et al. [21] utilized CDs as an initiator for doping into primary catalyst, and the carrier separation efficiency of the catalyst with CDs composite was significantly improved, and the doping of CDs increased the surface area of the catalyst and enhanced the photocatalytic ability. Luo et al. [22] demonstrated that photocatalysts integrated with CDs offer an abundance of binding sites for pollutants, further enhancing their effectiveness. Wei et al. [23] developed a composite material consisting of Ag nanoparticles/CDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays, showcasing superior photocatalytic activity due to the photoelectric properties of CDs. The incorporation of CDs into semiconductor photocatalysts emerges as a promising strategy to elevate the efficiency of photocatalytic processes.

In this study, we first fabricated positively charged CPC-CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The CPC-CDs were used to modify TiO2, resulting in the preparation of CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite materials. The aim of modifying TiO2 by the CPC-CDs was to reduce the bandgap energy of TiO2, thereby broadening its light-absorption range, and enhancing the electrostatic interaction between CPC-CDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI). The reduction performance of CPC-CDs/TiO2 for Cr(VI) was evaluated under different reaction conditions (CD content, catalyst dosage, pH, and initial substrate concentration). Furthermore, the reduction of Cr(VI) experiments and ion interference experiments were conducted to confirm the practical feasibility and stability of CPC-CDs/TiO2. On this basis, radical trapping experiments were performed to deduce the photocatalytic pathway and mechanism. The CPC-CDs/TiO2 proposed in this study aims to overcome the limitations of photocatalytic reduction under conditions of high concentration Cr(VI), neutral and alkaline environments, and natural sunlight, while also offers a valuable strategy for the reduction of Cr(VI) in real water environmental.

Hexadecylpyridinium chloride (CPC), titanium oxide (TiO2), ethylenediamine (EDA), absolute ethanol (EtOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), acetone, silver nitrate (AgNO3), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and p-benzoquinone (BQ), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline n-oxide (DMPO) and diphenylcarbazide (DPC) were purchased from AladdinCo. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli was obtained from the Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology and Health Effects, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China. Luria-Bertani (LB) Broth and agar were purchased from Hope Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1. All reagents were of analytical grade and required no further purification before use.

Using a modified one-step hydrothermal method based on previous research [20–24], we successfully synthesized CPC-CDs. Initially, 0.5 g of CPC and 100 μL of EDA were sonicated in 30.0 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 60 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate larger particles. The supernatant was then extracted, filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter, and subjected to an 8-h purification in deionized water using a dialysis membrane with a molecular cut-off of 500 Da. Finally, the solid CPC-CD product was collected by freeze-drying.

The CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was prepared by mixing TiO2 suspensions with solid CPC-CDs at three different concentrations (1–3 %). The resulting mixture was vigorously stirred with 20.0 mL of deionized water for 1 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined high-pressure autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. Following the hydrothermal reaction, the solution was centrifuged, washed twice with deionized water and ethanol, and freeze-dried for 48 h to collect the CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite material.

The photoreduction of Cr(VI) was conducted using a xenon lamp light source (PLS-SCE300D, Beijing, China) with an intensity equivalent to one sun (300 W). In a solution containing 50 mg/L of Cr(VI) and 100 mL volume, 0.1 g of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst was added. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium before initiating the photocatalytic reaction. The xenon light was then turned on, maintaining the distance between the light source and solution surface at approximately 15 cm. Aliquots of the Cr(VI) solution were collected at specific intervals. The solution was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the photocatalyst, followed by treatment using the DPC colorimetric method [25]. First, 1.0 mL of the filtered sample was mixed with 4.0 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 100.0 μL of 1 + 1 H2SO4 and 100 μL of 1 + 1 H3PO4 were added to the mixture and thoroughly mixed. Following this, 400.0 μL of freshly prepared DPC solution was added, and the solution was allowed to stand for 5–10 min for full color development. The remaining Cr(VI) concentration in the aqueous solution was determined via spectrophotometric conversion, by measuring the absorbance of the solution at 540 nm using deionized water as a reference. Additionally, the effects of various environmental conditions, such as the CD content, pH, initial concentration of Cr(VI), and photocatalyst dosage, on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) were explored.

Our experimental procedure involved conducting each trial with three independent replicates to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of our results. Each replicate was performed under identical conditions to minimize variability and to obtain robust and statistically significant data.

The antibacterial efficacy of CPC-CDs/TiO2 was assessed by deactivating Escherichia coli K-12 (TG1 strain). Suitable quantities of the agar medium and ultrapure water were heated and mixed thoroughly before being transferred and sealed in conical flasks. Subsequently, a 20-min pressure treatment at 121 °C and 0.01 MPa was conducted in an autoclave. Following treatment, the medium plates were cooled to 50 °C and placed upside down on a clean bench to ensure no adverse reactions before usage. For cell cultivation, the medium was incubated overnight at 37 °C on a shaker at 200 rpm in LB broth.

In the photocatalytic antibacterial experiments, bacterial cultures with a concentration of 1.0 × 105 CFU mL−1 were subjected to exposure under an intensity equivalent to that of a solar unit (80 W) from a xenon lamp light source. For each disinfection trial, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of physiological saline and placed in a transparent sterile container. Subsequently, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 60 min, followed by exposure to light at room temperature for 120 min. Every 30 min, 1 mL of the bacterial suspension was collected, diluted with physiological saline, and uniformly spread on LB agar plates. The plates were then cultured at 37 °C for 18 h, and the bacterial colony counts were determined by dilution coating plate method. Additionally, a control experiment was conducted in which bacterial samples were exposed to similar conditions, but without the addition of the photocatalyst.

TiO2, CPC-CDs, and CPC-CDs/TiO2 samples were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; IRTracer-100, Shimadzu, Japan). The surface composition and elemental states of CPC-CDs, CPC-CDs/TiO2 were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The valence band positions of TiO2 and CPC-CDs/TiO2 were analyzed by valence band XPS. To prepare CPC-CDs, TiO2, and CPC-CDs/TiO2 for microstructural observation, the samples were dispersed in ethanol via ultrasonication, drop-cast onto copper grids, dried, and placed on sample holders. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HT7700, Hitachi, Japan) images were obtained at 100 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM; S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) were conducted to inspect the morphologies and element of photocatalyst samples. X-ray Diffraction (XRD; Shimadzu XRD-7000, Japan) was employed to assess the crystallinity of the synthesized photocatalysts. The zetapotential values were recorded using a zetasizer (MalvernNano-Z) to measure the amount of charge of the photocatalysts. The ultrasound dispersion of Escherichia coli in absolute ethanol was performed before and after the reaction. The dispersed solution was placed on small silicon chips and adhered to sample holders using a conductive adhesive. After drying, the samples were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Sigma300, Carl Zeiss, Germany). UV–vis spectra of TiO2 and CPC-CDs/TiO2 were measured using ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS; UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra of liquid CPC-CDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded at room temperature using a Bruker ZMXmicro-6/1 spectrometer.

The microstructures of CPC-CDs, TiO2, and CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were observed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CPC-CDs particles had a monodisperse quasi-spherical shape with a narrow size distribution. Analysis of approximately 100 CPC-CDs particles indicated a particle-size distribution of 1.2–6.37 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 2.55 nm. The lattice spacing of the CPC-CDs are approximately 0.21 nm. The crystalline core in Fig. S1 exhibits a lattice plane distance of 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (101) lattice plane of anatase TiO2 [26]. Fig. 1b shows high-resolution TEM images of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite. In the same region, it is possible to observe the lattice spacings of CPC-CDs (0.21 nm) and TiO2 (0.35 nm), which even overlap in some regions. These findings show that the CDs fabricated by hydrothermal synthesis were successfully supported on the surface of the TiO2 nanoparticles. SEM images of TiO2 and CPC-CDs/TiO2 powders reveal no significant distinctions between the two samples (Fig. 1c). Both materials display morphological features characteristic of TiO2 nanoparticles, suggesting that the presence of CPC-CDs does not alter the fundamental nanoparticle structure. EDS analysis of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite, as illustrated in Fig. S2, reveals a discernible augmentation in the concentration of C, O and Ti elements when compared to pristine TiO2. This alteration unequivocally signifies the successful modification of the TiO2 surface by CPC-CDs.

To further verify the CPC-CDs structure, the chemical composition and bonding configuration of CPC-CDs were analyzed by XPS (Fig. 2 ). The XPS survey profiles (Fig. S3) indicate that CPC-CDs contains 90.6 % C, 4.54 % O, and 4.78 % N. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2a) exhibits three characteristic peaks at 284.75 eV (C–C/C = C), 283.82 eV(C–O/C = N) and 283.13 eV(C–O/C = N) [25]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b) displays two peaks at 531.85 and 529.99 eV, which are attributed to C–O/C = O and C = O bonds, respectively [27]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) was resolved into two binding energy peaks at 400.45 and 398.17 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen [25] and C = N–C [28], respectively. Thus, the above results demonstrate that the hydrothermal synthesis method successfully produced nitrogen-doped CDs. The XPS spectra of TiO2 are shown in Fig. S4. The O 1 s spectrum exhibits stretching vibration peaks at 530.05 and 528.42 eV, which are attributable to lattice oxygen and Ti = O bonds, respectively [29]. The Ti 2p spectrum displays four peaks at 463.01 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), 461.96 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 457.51 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2), and 457.15 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2) [30–32]. Fig. S5 shows the XPS spectra of CPC-CDs/ TiO2. The stretching vibration peaks of O and Ti of the CPC-CDs/ TiO2 are slightly offset. This shift suggests that the incorporation of CPC-CDs modifies the chemical environment on the TiO2 surface, leading to alterations in XPS peak positions. Notably, the emergence of N 1 s stretching vibration peaks at 401.07 and 399.06 eV in the CPC-CDs/ TiO2 spectrum corresponds to pyridinic N and N-H bonds, respectively [27]. This observation is due to the combination of CPC-CDs with TiO2 to form new chemical bonds and functional groups, thereby indicating that CPC-CDs may influence the band structure on the TiO2 surface and confirming the successful loading of CPC-CDs onto TiO2.

The crystal characteristics of the synthesized photocatalysts were analyzed using XRD. The powder XRD patterns of both TiO2 and the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite are depicted in Fig. S6. Remarkably, the XRD pattern of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite closely mirrors that of pristine TiO2, suggesting that the phase structure of TiO2 is preserved subsequent to the CPC-CDs deposition. The characteristic diffraction peaks of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite align well with those of pure TiO2, displaying peaks at 25.42°, 37.98°, 48.13°, 54.03°, 62.93°, 69.01°, 70.52°, and 75.26°. These peaks are indicative of reflections from the (101), (004), (200), (105), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystal facets, respectively [33]. Notably, a slight shift towards lower angles is observed in the XRD peaks of the composite, as highlighted in the inset of Fig. S6. Such shifts are indicative of the establishment of chemical and/or physical interactions between the CPC-CDs and the TiO2 matrix, subtly altering the lattice parameters [34].

The analysis of the optical properties showed that CPC-CDs formed colorless solutions in ambient light but emitted strong yellow fluorescence under irradiation with 312 and 365 nm UV light, with a maximum emission wavelength at 533 nm and a maximum excitation wavelength of 467 nm (Fig. S7). The FT-IR spectra of CPC-CDs, TiO2, and CPC-CDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 2d. The stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds [35] are observed at 3371.27, 3247.59, and 3245.70 cm−1 [25], indicating the sufficient water solubility of composite. Peaks at 2928.08 and 2915.21 cm−1 correspond to C − H bonds [36]. The characteristic peaks at 1637.13, 1636.52, and 1472.22 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C-N/C = N bonds [4]. For the TiO2 modified by CPC-CDs, a new C–H stretching vibration peak at 2928.08 cm−1 is observed, further confirming the successful formation of the composite.

Using UV–Vis DRS, we studied the optical properties of TiO2 and the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite. As depicted in Fig. 2f, pure TiO2 solely absorbs light below 361.01 nm, while the absorption edge of CPC-CDs/TiO2 extends from 361.01 to 384.12 nm. This delineates the crucial role of the CPC-CDs in broadening the light-responsive range of the photocatalyst. Based on the Tauc plot (Fig. 2g) and Eqs. (1) and (2) [37], the optical bandgap energy (E g) values of TiO2 and CPC-CDs/TiO2 were estimated. The E g of pure TiO2 (3.24 eV) is wider than that of CPC-CDs/TiO2 (3.12 eV).

Here, α is the optical absorption coefficient; A is a transition probability-dependent constant; c (3.0 × 108 m⋅s−1) is the speed of light; h (6.626 × 10−34 J⋅Hz−1) is Plank’s constant, and v is the radiation frequency.

Fig. 2h shows the XPS valence band (VB) spectrum of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite. Thus, the valence band energy (E VB) of the composite materials was determined to be 2.37 eV, which is nearly identical to that of TiO2 [38]. The conduction band (CB) energy (E CB = −0.75 eV) of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite was determined using the Mulliken electronegativity theory based on Eq. (3):

Based on the above results, it is evident that CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite possesses a narrower bandgap than pure TiO2. Consequently, they can excite electrons at lower energy levels, which should enable the reduction of Cr(VI) under sunlight irradiation.

Herein, the photocatalytic efficiency of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite was assessed for Cr(VI) reduction under sunlight irradiation (excitation wavelength > 365 nm) [39]. Fig. S8 depicts the changes in the Cr(VI) concentration over time (in the dark and under irradiation) in the presence of the as-synthesized photocatalyst. After 150 min of dark reaction and 150 min of sunlight irradiation, we observed approximately 19.85 % and 100 % reduction of Cr(VI), respectively. The photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) utilizing various light sources, including visible and ultraviolet light irradiation were investigated (Fig. S9). Under visible light irradiation for 150 min, CPC-CDs/TiO2 achieved a 50.13 % reduction of Cr(VI). Under ultraviolet light, the reduction rate reached 33.87 %. The higher reduction rate observed under sunlight irradiation, compared to that under visible and ultraviolet light, can be attributed to the specific characteristics of the photocatalyst and the nature of the light sources. Sunlight irradiation covers a broad spectrum of wavelengths, encompassing both visible and ultraviolet light, thus providing a more comprehensive energy source for photocatalytic reactions [40]. Sunlight irradiation ensures the activation of photocatalysts across a wide range of energy levels, facilitating a more efficient reduction of Cr(VI).

To reveal the effect of the CPC-CDs loading on the catalytic activity toward Cr(VI) reduction, we compared the reduction efficiencies of TiO2 and CPC-CDs/TiO2 (1 %, 2 %, 3 % and 4 %). As shown in Fig. 3 a, under dark conditions, all as-prepared samples achieved Cr(VI) adsorption equilibrium in 30 min. Owing to the notable limitations of TiO2, such as its poor light absorption capability and rapid recombination of e−–h+ pairs [41], pure TiO2 exhibits a reduction rate of only 50 % towards Cr(VI). In contrast, the CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits outstanding photocatalytic reduction performance. As the CPC-CDs doping level increased up to 3 %, the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) by the CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was remarkably enhanced and nearly 100 % of the Cr(VI) was reduced. This indicates that the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite catalysts provide more adsorption sites for pollutant reactions.

The photocatalytic reduction kinetics of the photocatalyst followed the pseudo-first-order kinetics model (Eq. (4)):
(4)
- l n ( C /
C 0
) = k t
where C and C 0 are the concentration of residual Cr(VI) at time t and time 0, respectively, and k represents the rate constant of the kinetic reaction (min−1). Fig. 3b shows the linear kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction across the photocatalyst. The kinetic curves of all catalysts were linear, all fitted P-values were less than 0.05, and all R 2 values were greater than 90 %, indicating that the pseudo-first-order kinetic model had a good fit with the experimental data (Table S3). Our findings suggest that CPC-CDs modification enhances the catalytic performance of TiO2, with the 3 % CPC-CDs (0.03231 min−1) loading demonstrating the most pronounced improvement (Fig. 3b). The decrease in reduction efficiency observed at 4 % CPC-CDs (0.01996 min−1) loading may be attributed to the detrimental impact of excessive CPC-CDs loading on the catalytic performance of the photocatalyst. The difference in photocatalytic effect between 2 % and 3 % is not obvious, considering both effectiveness and cost-effectiveness, we opted for the 2 % CPC-CDs loading in subsequent experiments.

To further refine the catalytic conditions, we explored the effects of varying the photocatalyst dosage (0–0.15 g/100 mL). Increasing the photocatalyst dosage had a favorable impact on the catalytic reaction (Fig. 3c). The reduction efficiency increased notably with increasing photocatalyst concentration in the range of 0–0.15 g/100 mL. Reduction rates of up to 100 % occurred with photocatalyst dosages of both 0.1 and 0.15 g/100 mL. Fig. 3d shows the pseudo-first-order kinetic model for various photocatalyst doses. A Cr(VI) reduction rate of 0.02529 min−1 was obtained for a photocatalyst loading of 0.1 g/100 mL, which is 9.37 and 1.66 times higher than those for 0.05 g/100 mL (0.0027 min−1) and 0.15 g/100 mL (0.01524 min−1) (Table S4). Therefore, the 0.1 g/100 mL photocatalyst dosage is considered optimal.

Diagrams of the different initial pH values of Cr(VI) solution (pH = 3, 5, 7, and 9) are shown in Fig. 3e. The speciation and migration of Cr in aqueous systems are influenced by pH and the presence of oxidizing agents [37–39,41,42]. According to the Purboix curve for Cr(VI), the predominant form of Cr(VI) in aqueous solutions is H2CrO4 (aq.) and
HCrO
4
-
below pH 6.5, shifting to
CrO
4
2-
above pH 6.5 [43,44]. As shown in the diagram, the fastest reaction rate was observed at pH 7. Cr(VI) can be completely reduced under acidic conditions; even under alkaline conditions, the reduction rate can reach 97.35 % within 420 min. This is attributed to an increase in the negative surface charge on 2 % CPC-CDs/TiO2 with increasing pH, which enhances the electrostatic repulsion between the photocatalyst and
Cr 2 O
7
2-
. Consequently, the migration of Cr(VI) toward the photocatalyst surface is inhibited, leading to a lower Cr (VI) removal rate. Another significant factor may be the increased stability of Cr(VI) under alkaline conditions. Fig. 3f shows the first-order dynamic equations corresponding to different pH values. The reduction rate of Cr (VI) is 0.02727 min−1 at pH = 7, which is 1.59, 2.07, and 3.75 times higher than that at pH = 3 (0.01707 min−1), pH = 5 (0.01316 min−1), and pH = 9 (0.00726 min−1), respectively (Table S5). All R 2 values were greater than 95 %, indicating that the equations fit the experimental data well. This illustrates that 2 % CPC-CDs/TiO2 effectively reduces Cr(VI) over a wide pH range.

The effects of the initial concentration of Cr(VI) (10–60 mg/L) on the photocatalytic reduction activity were also studied. As shown in Fig. 3g, under identical photocatalyst dosages, reaction temperatures, and solution pH conditions, the time of reduction Cr(VI) is directly proportional to its initial concentration. The first-order reduction rate constants of Cr(VI) at different initial concentrations were determined by linear regression (Fig. 3h), and the derived rate constants are listed in Table S6. At a concentration of 10 mg/L, the Cr(VI) reduction rate is 0.38643 min−1, which is 6.57, 7.62, 14.90, 15.12, and 92.45 times higher than the rates observed at 20 mg/L (0.05878 min−1), 30 mg/L (0.05069 min−1), 40 mg/L (0.02593 min−1), 50 mg/L (0.02556 min−1), and 60 mg/L (0.00418 min−1). High initial Cr(VI) concentrations significantly affected the reduction reaction by hindering photon penetration into the photocatalyst reaction interface, resulting in fewer photoactive species. This scenario intensifies the competition between pollutants and their intermediate products for the active sites on the photocatalyst [45,46].

From a practical perspective, the stability and reusability of photocatalysts have attracted widespread attention in terms of cost-effectiveness and environmental concerns. To further estimate the practicability of CPC-CDs/TiO2, five regeneration cycles of the photocatalyst were conducted by simple water washing and oven drying. As shown in Fig. 6a, although the photocatalyst was used for five cycles, a consistent Cr(VI) reduction rate of 93.57 % was achieved, indicating the excellent reusability of the prepared CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst. Following the reduction of Cr(VI), the FT-IR curve of CPC-CDs/TiO2 did not change significantly after cycling (Fig. 2d). The XRD pattern of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite exhibits substantial consistency with its pre-reaction state (Fig. S10). Consequently, this stability implies the photocatalyst's potential for recycling and reuse post-recovery. Fig. S11 illustrates the SEM images of CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite before and after five photocatalytic cycles, revealing negligible changes in surface morphology, indicative of the material's structural stability through repeated reductions. Fig. S12 shows that the EDS analysis of the photocatalyst before and after cycling shows slight increases in the content of Cr elements after cycling. This phenomenon probably because the adsorption of Cr ions onto the material surface. The above results demonstrates its excellent regenerability and durability.

The excellent antibacterial properties of photocatalysts can improve their practical applicability. The CPC-CDs prepared in this study contain quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs) with cationic groups, which improve their antibacterial properties [47]. To evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite material, Escherichia coli was selected as the target organism for photocatalytic antibacterial experiments. The results for the control group without the addition of the photocatalyst are shown in Fig. 4 a. There was no significant change in bacterial colony density after 30 min of darkness, followed by 60 min of continual irradiation. This indicates that the experimental temperature and sunlight intensity did not affect bacterial reproduction. After the introduction of the photocatalyst, there was no noticeable reduction in bacterial colony density after 30 min in the dark. Subsequently, after 30 and 60 min of irradiation, significant inhibition of bacterial reproduction was observed. In the dark, CPC-CDs/TiO2 does not generate sufficient ROS with antibacterial properties. However, under sunlight illumination, CPC-CDs/TiO2 generates a substantial amount of radicals, thereby inhibiting Escherichia coli reproduction. SEM images of the morphology of Escherichia coli in the solution before and after the reaction (Fig. 4b,c) show a considerable decrease in the number of bacteria with visible damage on their surfaces caused by the photocatalyst.

As shown in Fig. 2e, TiO2 has a zeta potential of 1.87 mV, which increases to 3.93 mV for CPC-CDs/TiO2 after loading with the CDs owing to the presence of positively charged surface functional groups. Cr(VI) has a negative charge, which allows it to become highly dispersed in water-based solvents. After contact with Cr(VI), electrostatic interactions occur between CPC-CDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI), as Cr(VI) readily attaches to the active sites on the surface of the photocatalytic, thereby reducing its zeta potential. These findings further confirm that the synthesized CPC-CDs have a positive charge and can effectively modify TiO2, consistent with the results of the reduction experiments.

Based on the experimental results, it is clear that incorporating CPC-CDs into TiO2 leads to the enhanced photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in an aqueous medium. This performance enhancement is attributed to the extended light-absorption range and reduced bandgap energy of the composite compared to pure TiO2. As shown in Fig. 2f–h, incorporating CPC-CDs into TiO2 significantly extended the light absorption edge, thus improving visible-light harvesting. The electronic coupling of the CPC-CDs and the CB of TiO2 induces a narrower nanohybrid bandgap than that of pure TiO2, resulting in a reduced bandgap energy [48].

Fig. 5 a shows the equilibrium sorption capacity (Q e) as a function of the initial Cr(VI) concentration. At equal initial Cr(VI) concentrations, the Q e of CPC-CDs/TiO2 exceeded that of TiO2, indicating that the CDs significantly enhanced the sorption capacity. To accurately represent the sorption isotherms, we applied two well-known isotherm models: the Langmuir model (Eq. (5)) and Freundlich (Eq. (6)) [49,50].
(5)
Q e
=
Q
max
×
K L
×
C e
1 +
K L
×
C e
(6)

=
K F
×
C e
1 / n
Here, Q max is the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, C e is the equilibrium concentration of Cr(VI) ions, K L and K F are the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption constants, respectively, n is the heterogeneity factor, and R 2 indicates the linear regression coefficients obtained from the fits of the experimental data. The fitting results are shown in Fig. 5b and 5c, and the corresponding parameters are summarized in Table S1. The Q max of CPC-CDs/TiO2, as determined by the Langmuir model, is 12.97 mg/g, which is approximately 1.76 times higher than that of TiO2 (7.35 mg/g). According to the R 2 values, the Freundlich model provided the best description of the Cr(VI) adsorption behavior of CPC-CDs/TiO2 and TiO2, implying a monolayer adsorption process [51]. Fig. 5d shows the effect of the contact time on the adsorption capacity (Q t). The Q t of CPC-CDs/TiO2 surpasses that of TiO2 at identical contact times (t), indicating that CDs loading enhances the Cr(VI) adsorption rate. To fit the experimental data, a pseudo-first-order model (Eq. (7)) was employed [50]:
(7)

=
Q e
( 1 - e x p
( - k t )
)
where k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant. The fitting results are presented in Fig. 5e and 5f, and the corresponding parameters are summarized in Table S2. According to the R 2 values, Cr(VI) adsorption by CPC-CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 was best described by the pseudo-second-order model [52]. This indicates that the addition of CPC-CDs improves the adsorption performance of TiO2.

To determine the possible mechanisms of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst toward Cr(VI) reduction, free-radical quenching experiments were conducted to determine the main reactive species in the reduction. Specifically, AgNO3, EDTA, IPA, and BQ were used as scavengers for h+, e−, •OH, and •
O
2
-
, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 b, EDTA and IPA had minimal effects on Cr(VI) reduction, which suggests that e− and •OH are not the main active species contributing to the reduction. In comparison, 51.44 % and 70.13 % of Cr(VI) was reduced when BQ and AgNO3 were added to the system, respectively, suggesting that •
O
2
-
and e− generated in the system are mainly responsible for Cr(VI) reduction.

The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CPC-CDs/TiO2 was mainly attributed to the separation of •
O
2
-
, e−, h+, and •OH. The reduction capability of CPC-CDs/TiO2 primarily originates from the presence of effective active species, with a minor contribution from e-–h+ pair separation. To further verify the results of the quenching experiment, EPR measurements were conducted under simulated sunlight to identify free radical species. The characteristic quartets with intensities of 1:1:1:1 correspond to DMPO–•
O
2
-
. In Fig. 6c, consistent with the results of the active-species capture experiments, the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite shows a strong DMPO-•
O
2
-
signal. The EPR spectrum indicates the generation of •
O
2
-
by CPC-CDs/TiO2 under sunlight irradiation.

To elucidate the behaviors related to the separation and migration of photogenerated charge carriers within the photocatalysts, analyses via transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed [53]. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, pristine TiO2 displayed the lower photocurrent intensity, in contrast to the CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, which exhibited a markedly higher intensity. This augmentation in photocurrent intensity is indicative of an improved efficiency in the separation and migration rates of charge carriers [54,55]. Such findings suggest that doping with CPC-CDs effectively diminishes electron-hole recombination, thereby significantly enhancing the photoactivity of the nanocomposites. The EIS results, depicted in Fig. 6f, further corroborate these observations. Conventionally, each arc in the plot signifies the resistance encountered during charge transfer, with smaller arc radii implying reduced charge transfer resistance [56,57]. The CPC-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits a smaller arc radius in comparison to pristine TiO2, indicating that the composite possesses lower charge transfer resistance and more efficient charge separation.”.

Furthermore, XPS spectra were analyzed to determine the synergistic mechanism of enhanced reduction and photocatalysis by CPC-CDs/TiO2. Fig. 6d shows the XPS survey spectrum of CPC-CDs/TiO2. Before reduction, CPC-CDs/TiO2 contained only Ti, O, C, and N in proportions of 35.55 %, 55.88 %, 8.24 %, and 0.33 %, respectively. After five reduction cycles, CPC-CDs/TiO2 contained Ti, O, C, N, and Cr in proportions of 11.69 %, 37.82 %, 47.48 %, 1.67 %, and 1.34 %, respectively. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of CPC-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. S5a) reveals deconvoluted peaks at 530.69 eV (Ti–O bonds) and 529.24 eV (Ti = O bonds) before the photocatalytic reaction. After the reaction (Fig. 6g), peaks are observed at 530.39 eV (Ti–O bonds), 528.84 eV (Ti = O bonds), and 531.99 eV (O–H bonds) [58]. The shift towards lower binding energies for both the Ti–O and Ti = O peaks indicated partial oxygen reduction and surface redox reactions [59]. Fig. S5b illustrates the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum. Three fitted peaks are observed at 287.62, 284.92, and 283.71 eV, corresponding to C = O, C–O/C–N, and C–C/C = C bonds, respectively [60,61]. After the photocatalytic reduction reaction (Fig. 6g), all three peaks shift towards higher binding energies, indicating electron transfer due to photocatalysis. The high-resolution Cr 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 6h) of CPC-CDs/TiO2 displays two components each for Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2 orbitals at 579.69/576.74 eV and 590.42/586.04 eV, attributed to Cr(VI) and Cr(III) ions, respectively [62]. The Cr(III) peaks confirm the reduction of Cr(VI) ions. Fig. 6i shows high-resolution N 1 s spectra after the reduction reaction. Before the reaction (Fig. S5c), the XPS bands at 401.07 and 399.06 eV correspond to pyridine nitrogen and N–H, respectively [27], with a noticeable increase in the intensity of bands related to pyridine nitrogen after reduction. This result confirmed the enhanced role of N in Cr(VI) reduction [63]. Furthermore, the Ti 2p spectrum of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 composite (Fig. S5d) displays four peaks at 458.01 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2), 458.19 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2), 463.38 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), and 464.17 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2) [30–32]. The change in the binding energy between Ti3+ and Ti4+ may be attributed to the generation of electrons due to the breakage of Ti bonds. This indicates the involvement of Ti in the reduction reaction (Fig. 6i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reaction and ion interference experiments under sunlight
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of CPC-CDs/TiO2 as a photocatalyst, we conducted photocatalytic reduction experiments under sunlight irradiation. The environmental parameters included a temperature of 23 °C, humidity of 40 %, and UV index of 5. Considering the actual concentration of Cr(VI) in the environment, we selected a reaction system containing 10 mg/L Cr(VI) and added 0.1 g of 2 % CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst. The results indicated that under real sunlight exposure, CPC-CDs/TiO2 achieved 99.32 % reduction of Cr(VI) within 30 min. This highlights the potential of CPC-CDs/TiO2 as a cost-efficient photocatalyst for the reduction of Cr(VI) under practical environmental conditions.

To examine the effects of other common ions, we evaluated Cr(VI) reduction using the same reaction system under sunlight. We conducted ion-interference experiments by introducing interference from common metal ions (10 mg/L) to evaluate the stability of CPC-CDs/TiO2 based on the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI). As depicted in Fig. S13 and S15, the addition of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, NO3 − and NO2 − to the reaction system resulted in outstanding Cr(VI) reduction efficiency within 30 min. Fig. S14 and S16 illustrates the first-order kinetic equations corresponding to the reduction efficiencies of different ions, showing a linear model (P < 0.05) (Table S8). This suggests a good fit between the pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the experimental data, indicating that the CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst is resistant to interference from anions and cations, to demonstrates significant potential for application.

To explore the potential of CPC-CDs/TiO2 for environmental remediation, our study conducted Cr(VI) reduction experiments at three different times on the same day to compare the reduction performance of the photocatalysts (Fig. S17). The capability to completely reduce a 10 mg/L concentration of Cr(VI) within 30 min under sunlight at both 10:00 AM and 2:00 PM, followed by a decreased reduction rate requiring 35 min at 6:00 PM, suggests a dependency of photocatalytic activity on sunlight intensity [64].

To highlight the performance of CPC-CDs/TiO2, we compared it with existing studies. As shown in Table S7, most studies demonstrated satisfactory reduction efficiencies only at low concentrations of Cr(VI) (10 and 20 mg/L). In contrast, our catalyst achieved the complete reduction of Cr(VI), even at a high concentration of 50 mg/L. While many studies opt for acidic conditions, as the abundance of hydrogen ion under acidic conditions facilitates the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), CPC-CDs/TiO2 proved versatile across a wide pH range (pH 3, 5, 7, and 9). Finally, in comparison to other materials tested under laboratory light sources, CPC-CDs/TiO2 achieved the nearly complete reduction of 10 mg/L Cr(VI) with a 99.32 % reduction rate under natural sunlight exposure, demonstrates its greater potential for practical applications. Based on the above, the CPC-CDs/TiO2 exhibit greater competitiveness in Cr(VI) reduction.

In this study we introduce a novel approach, TiO2 was modified with CPC-CDs to develop a versatile photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction in water. The CPC-CDs enhance the visible light absorption, electron transfer capability, and carrier separation efficiency of TiO2. The effective proximity between CPC-CDs/TiO2 and Cr(VI) is increased through electrostatic interactions, consequently enhancing the reduction efficiency. The enhanced performance of the CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst is attributed to the synergistic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis. This enhancement significantly improves the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency under a range of initial Cr(VI) concentrations, pH, and light exposure conditions. The CPC-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst achieved the complete reduction of 50 mg/L of high-concentration Cr(VI) within 150 min under neutral pH and simulated sunlight. Remarkably, it consistently maintained reduction rates above 95 % across the pH range of 3–9. Furthermore, CPC-CDs/TiO2 demonstrated promising practical applications, achieving the complete reduction of 10 mg/L Cr(VI) in 30 min under natural sunlight and maintaining high efficiency (93.57 % reduction) over five cycles, indicating exceptional catalytic stability. Additionally, CPC-CDs/TiO2 exhibits notable antibacterial properties against Escherichia coli. This study presents a highly efficient photocatalytic material for Cr(VI) reduction with practical applications in real environmental scenarios, offering valuable insights and technical solutions for the treatment of Cr(VI)-contaminated water bodies.
